chapter_summary: Ruru asks a sage how the kshatriya Janamejaya came to harm the serpents, why the serpents (pannagas) were destroyed and by whom they were rescued, and begs to hear the great account of Astika in full. The sage replies that Ruru will hear the whole great deed of Astika as recited by the brahmanas, and then vanishes from sight. Ruru runs all over the forest searching for the vanished sage, until, exhausted, he collapses to the ground. On regaining consciousness he returns and reports the encounter to his father, who, when asked, narrates the entire story to him.

story.title: Ruru Seeks the Tale of Astika
story.arc: Ruru questions a sage about how Janamejaya destroyed the serpents and how they were saved, longing to hear the full story of Astika. The sage promises he will hear it from the brahmanas and then vanishes. Ruru frantically chases the disappeared sage through the forest until he collapses from exhaustion. Recovering, he returns to his father, who narrates the whole account to him.
story.timeline_note: A framing episode: Ruru's curiosity sets up the forthcoming narration of the Astika legend (the snake-sacrifice cycle) by his father.
story.comic_potential: Ruru sprinting in circles all over the forest chasing a sage who has already vanished into thin air, then face-planting from exhaustion, is an unintentionally slapstick image of over-eager devotion.

teachings.teaching: Sacred knowledge is not seized by force or frantic pursuit; the vanished sage cannot be caught by running, and the answer instead comes calmly through the proper channel of the father and the brahmanas.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework, truth withdraws from grasping effort: the seeker who chases the higher reality through outward agitation collapses, while realization arrives only when one returns to the lineage (guru/father) and receives it in stillness rather than chasing it.

teachings.teaching: The desire to hear the deeds of Astika completely models the receptive humility of the true student, who asks and then listens in full rather than presuming to already know.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma, the disciple's wholehearted wish to receive the teaching 'in its entirety' (asheshatah) is the proper inner posture for transmission; knowledge of the deathless principle is given to the one who genuinely yearns to hear it.
